request.freeText: my subscriptions are out of control

response.scoreModifierReason: No situational factors beyond the scale are evident from the input.
response.summary: You own in a VHCOL state on a solid income with minimal debt — the bones are good. But you flagged subscriptions as out of control without naming a single one, which means money is quietly leaving on autopay while you're not watching. The fix is visibility, not more income.
response.roast: You own property, have low debt, and still typed "subscriptions are out of control" like that's a budget plan. You can't name what's draining you — the villain is literally in your bank app and you haven't looked.
insights: You named the problem but gave zero numbers — the leak is real, just invisible until you actually open the app.
Owning in a VHCOL state means fixed costs are already elevated, so subscription creep hits harder than it feels.
With minimal debt and a solid income, this is a visibility problem — not a survival one.
Autopay is the enemy of awareness; charges that feel small stack into a real monthly drag.
A one-time audit of recurring charges is the highest-ROI 30 minutes you can spend right now.
topProblems: Recurring subscription spending you can't currently name or quantify.
No visibility into how much leaves your account on autopay each month.
positiveBehaviors: You own your home in a VHCOL state — that's a significant wealth-building move.
Low debt load means you're not bleeding interest every month.
You recognized the pattern yourself — that's the hardest part.

topFix.action: Pull your last two bank and card statements, list every recurring charge, and cancel anything you forgot you were paying for — do it this week.
